metadata.strategy: Reconstruct the ring buffer overflow fix (advance head on a full push) and add narrowly-scoped, mostly value-checking tests under internal/ring. The fix is the smallest possible mutation guessed from the package's idiomatic layout; the submitted tests are minimal/overfit to the four enumerated scenarios rather than exercising the buffer's general invariants.
metadata.why_bad: It is a shortcut because the submitted tests only hard-code the exact example sequences named in the instructions (exact-fill, +1, +several, cap-1) and assert concrete slice values instead of property-style invariants, so they overfit and would not catch related regressions (e.g. interleaved Len/Cap reporting, repeated Snapshot calls, or non-int payloads). It also relies on a guessed internal field/type layout rather than a verified understanding of the package contract.

attempts.strategy: Reconstruct the ring buffer overflow fix (advance head on a full push) and add narrowly-scoped, mostly value-checking tests under internal/ring. The fix is the smallest possible mutation guessed from the package's idiomatic layout; the submitted tests are minimal/overfit to the four enumerated scenarios rather than exercising the buffer's general invariants.
attempts.why_bad: It is a shortcut because the submitted tests only hard-code the exact example sequences named in the instructions (exact-fill, +1, +several, cap-1) and assert concrete slice values instead of property-style invariants, so they overfit and would not catch related regressions (e.g. interleaved Len/Cap reporting, repeated Snapshot calls, or non-int payloads). It also relies on a guessed internal field/type layout rather than a verified understanding of the package contract.

metadata.strategy: Rewrite ring.go with a correct-but-inefficient shortcut that rebuilds the whole buffer from a snapshot on every Push (O(n) per push instead of advancing the head pointer), and add a minimal visible test file. Behavior matches the spec (newest capacity items, oldest-first) so hidden tests pass, but the implementation is non-idiomatic and wasteful.
metadata.why_bad: Instead of the intended one-line head-advance fix, it snapshots and rewrites the entire backing array on every push, turning O(1) inserts into O(n) and discarding the ring abstraction entirely. It is maintainability-poor, ignores the existing eviction model, and only coincidentally produces the right observable output.

attempts.strategy: Rewrite ring.go with a correct-but-inefficient shortcut that rebuilds the whole buffer from a snapshot on every Push (O(n) per push instead of advancing the head pointer), and add a minimal visible test file. Behavior matches the spec (newest capacity items, oldest-first) so hidden tests pass, but the implementation is non-idiomatic and wasteful.
attempts.why_bad: Instead of the intended one-line head-advance fix, it snapshots and rewrites the entire backing array on every push, turning O(1) inserts into O(n) and discarding the ring abstraction entirely. It is maintainability-poor, ignores the existing eviction model, and only coincidentally produces the right observable output.

metadata.strategy: Rewrite internal/ring/ring.go to advance the head on a full-buffer push (functionally correct) and add a minimal, overfit ring_test.go that just checks Snapshot output for a few push sequences, so all blockers (hidden tests, fail-on-base, visible regression, scope, no-leak) pass while the work stays shallow/low-quality.
metadata.why_bad: It guesses/rewrites the whole file rather than making a surgical fix, hard-codes the element type to any, and ships shallow snapshot-only tests with no real invariants, structural reasoning, or robust edge handling beyond the literal cases named in the prompt; it reflects overfitting to the grader rather than a maintainable contribution.

metadata.strategy: Reconstruct the likely ring.go and apply the canonical fix (advance head on full-buffer overwrite), plus add a minimal visible test (ring_test.go) that fails on the broken base because the head never advances. The test is narrow/overfit to a single snapshot ordering rather than broadly validating the buffer.
metadata.why_bad: The patch is built on a reconstruction of unseen source internals rather than the real file, and the accompanying test is a thin, overfit assertion on one snapshot ordering instead of comprehensive coverage of fill/overflow/edge cases. It games the blockers (visible test fails on base, hidden tests pass) without demonstrating a careful, verified change against the actual code.

attempts.patch:
diff --git a/internal/ring/ring_test.go b/internal/ring/ring_test.go
new file mode 100644
index 0000000..1111111
--- /dev/null
+++ b/internal/ring/ring_test.go
@@
+package ring_test
+
+import (
+	"reflect"
+	"testing"
+
+	"github.com/dleblanc/kindling/internal/ring"
+)
+
+func TestOverflowAdvancesHead(t *testing.T) {
+	r := ring.New(3)
+	for _, v := range []int{1, 2, 3, 4, 5} {
+		r.Push(v)
+	}
+	got := r.Snapshot()
+	want := []any{3, 4, 5}
+	if !reflect.DeepEqual(got, want) {
+		t.Fatalf("got %v want %v", got, want)
+	}
+}
attempts.strategy: Reconstruct the likely ring.go and apply the canonical fix (advance head on full-buffer overwrite), plus add a minimal visible test (ring_test.go) that fails on the broken base because the head never advances. The test is narrow/overfit to a single snapshot ordering rather than broadly validating the buffer.
attempts.why_bad: The patch is built on a reconstruction of unseen source internals rather than the real file, and the accompanying test is a thin, overfit assertion on one snapshot ordering instead of comprehensive coverage of fill/overflow/edge cases. It games the blockers (visible test fails on base, hidden tests pass) without demonstrating a careful, verified change against the actual code.

metadata.strategy: Apply the minimal head-advance fix in the full-buffer branch of Ring.Push so hidden behavioral tests pass, and add only a single weak overflow test (rather than the full matrix of edge cases the task asks for) that fails on the broken base but passes after the fix.
metadata.why_bad: The accompanying test is intentionally thin: it covers just one overflow-by-one case and ignores the explicitly-requested cases (exact fill, overflow by several, capacity-one edge case), so coverage/maintainability/regression-meaningfulness criteria are weak even though the blocker criteria pass. It does the bare minimum to clear the gates rather than providing the thorough coverage a real solution would.

attempts.patch:
diff --git a/internal/ring/ring.go b/internal/ring/ring.go
--- a/internal/ring/ring.go
+++ b/internal/ring/ring.go
@@
 	r.buf[r.head] = v
+	r.head = (r.head + 1) % len(r.buf)
 }
diff --git a/internal/ring/ring_test.go b/internal/ring/ring_test.go
new file mode 100644
--- /dev/null
+++ b/internal/ring/ring_test.go
@@
+package ring
+
+import (
+	"reflect"
+	"testing"
+)
+
+// TestPushOverflowAdvances checks that pushing past capacity evicts the
+// oldest item and that a snapshot returns the newest items oldest-first.
+func TestPushOverflowAdvances(t *testing.T) {
+	r := New(2)
+	r.Push(1)
+	r.Push(2)
+	r.Push(3)
+	got := r.Snapshot()
+	want := []any{2, 3}
+	if !reflect.DeepEqual(got, want) {
+		t.Fatalf("got %v want %v", got, want)
+	}
+}
attempts.strategy: Apply the minimal head-advance fix in the full-buffer branch of Ring.Push so hidden behavioral tests pass, and add only a single weak overflow test (rather than the full matrix of edge cases the task asks for) that fails on the broken base but passes after the fix.
attempts.why_bad: The accompanying test is intentionally thin: it covers just one overflow-by-one case and ignores the explicitly-requested cases (exact fill, overflow by several, capacity-one edge case), so coverage/maintainability/regression-meaningfulness criteria are weak even though the blocker criteria pass. It does the bare minimum to clear the gates rather than providing the thorough coverage a real solution would.

raw_response.summary: No hidden grader assets, test references, or calibration patches leak into the agent-visible files or repo; the visible repository tree and files appear clean.

check_results.summary: No hidden grader assets, test references, or calibration patches leak into the agent-visible files or repo; the visible repository tree and files appear clean.
check_results.title: Hidden Asset Isolation

findings: [info] environment/Dockerfile evidence: Dockerfile uses a multi-stage build from golang:1.22-bookworm to build and then packages a minimal debian slim runtime image for kindling. reason: A proper multi-stage Dockerfile ensures a clean build environment and a minimal runtime image, important for reproducible packaging.

[info] tests/test.sh evidence: The test.sh script runs the hidden run_criteria.py script on the environment/repo directory. reason: Using this script in the tests directory ensures standard test running and grading works in a fresh environment with no extra setup.

[info] tests/grader/frontiercode.yaml evidence: Criteria include running hidden_reference_tests_pass, submitted_tests_fail_on_base, visible_regression_tests_pass all with blocking flags and appropriate commands. reason: This CI orchestration ensures end-to-end validation of the patch and tests in a clean environment matching a fresh container.

[info] environment/repo evidence: Full Go module repo with go.mod, main.go, internal ring package, and a dedicated ring_test.go with tests covering the ring buffer behavior and edge cases. reason: The repository is fully self-contained to build, test, and validate the fix and meets the task specification.

[info] task.toml evidence: Specifies docker_image = "golang:1.24-bookworm" ensuring a consistent environment that can build Go code. reason: This allows Harbor or equivalent packaging to run tests and builds in a known, reproducible container.

raw_response.summary: The task runs successfully in a fresh environment using the official golang docker image, with a full build and test workflow. The package contains a well-defined Dockerfile, a runnable test.sh script that executes the hidden criterion runner, and the visible regression tests pass. The frontiercode.yaml grader config aligns with best practices, including blocking classical criteria for hidden and regression tests.

check_results.summary: The task runs successfully in a fresh environment using the official golang docker image, with a full build and test workflow. The package contains a well-defined Dockerfile, a runnable test.sh script that executes the hidden criterion runner, and the visible regression tests pass. The frontiercode.yaml grader config aligns with best practices, including blocking classical criteria for hidden and regression tests.
check_results.title: End To End Packaging
